summary: This article proposes N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) as a therapeutic agent to reduce intestinal permeability in patients with Short Bowel Syndrome (SBS). It details a proposed protocol, mechanism of action, and summarizes existing evidence, aiming to establish prior art. The submission is well-researched and grounded in scientific literature, though it acknowledges the lack of direct clinical trials in SBS patients.
strengths: Comprehensive review of existing literature and mechanistic pathways.
Well-structured and clearly written, making a complex topic accessible.
Proactive approach to establishing prior art for a potential therapeutic use.
Thoroughly details the proposed protocol, including dosage, monitoring, and contraindications.
weaknesses: Lack of direct clinical trial data specifically for NAC in SBS patients.
The proposed protocol is an extrapolation from studies on other gastrointestinal conditions.
Reliance on preclinical and in vitro studies for mechanistic support.
suggestions: Emphasize the need for a dedicated clinical trial to validate the proposed protocol in SBS patients.
Further elaborate on the potential challenges in NAC's oral bioavailability and metabolism in SBS patients.
Consider including a brief discussion on alternative or complementary therapies for SBS that might synergize with NAC.

claims.text: N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) is the N-acetyl derivative of the amino acid L-cysteine and serves as a direct precursor to the intracellular antioxidant glutathione (GSH).

SBS patients have elevated circulating zonulin (47.24 ± 2.14 vs. 39.48 ± 1.20 ng/mL in controls), elevated LBP (30.32 ± 13.25 vs. 9.77 ± 0.71 µg/mL), increased soluble CD14, and reduced fatty acid binding protein 2 (FABP-2) — all markers of intestinal barrier damage [2].
NAC group: 6.1% endoscopic relapse vs. 26.7% placebo (p=0.001); reduced fecal calprotectin, ESR, hs-CRP [4].

As of the publication date of this disclosure, no granted patent or published application was identified that specifically claims the method of administering N-acetylcysteine for the reduction of intestinal permeability in short bowel syndrome.
claims.source: Google Patents Search: 'N-acetylcysteine intestinal permeability short bowel syndrome'

novelty_vs_existing.title: N-Acetylcysteine Compositions and Methods
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission focuses on the therapeutic use-case of NAC for intestinal permeability in SBS, whereas the cited patent is primarily concerned with formulation and storage-stable IV compositions, not this specific method of use.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Methods of Treating Conditions Associated with Leaky Gut Barrier
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission specifically identifies NAC as the active agent for reducing intestinal permeability in SBS, supported by mechanistic and preclinical data. The cited patent covers broader probiotic and dietary supplement compositions for leaky gut without singling out NAC for this specific indication.
